data.title: The Climate Contribution Framework methodology
data.subtitle: The Climate Contribution Framework (CCF) does not replace existing methodologies. It builds on them — connecting heterogeneous scores, ratings, and certifications into a single, fair, and comparable indicator of corporate contribution to global net zero.

data.title: Three pillars. One equation. // Sector-adjusted results.
data.subtitle: Three non-substitutable pillars, sector-weighted and combined into a single score. No pillar compensates for others. The sum α + β + γ represents the sector's overall climate materiality — between 10 and 100.

data.title: A, B, C — Pillar scores (0–100)
data.subtitle: Each pillar produces a normalized score between 0 and 100. It reflects the company's actual performance, adjusted for data quality and the rigor of the source framework.

data.title: α, β, γ — Sector coefficients
data.subtitle: Three coefficients calibrated for each sector. They determine the relative weight of each pillar based on the actual climate materiality of the company's activity.

data.title: α + β + γ — Sector contribution potential
data.subtitle: The sum of coefficients, between 10 and 100, indicates how far a sector can act on climate. A potential of 96 means maximum responsibility and capacity for action.

data.contentText: This architecture ensures that companies are evaluated on the most material levers for their business model, while preserving cross-sector comparability.

data.title: Reduction
data.subtitle: Carbon footprint reduction

data.text: Pillar A evaluates how companies decarbonize their operations and value chains across Scopes 1, 2, and 3, as well as:
Each is weighted and aggregated into a single pillar score.

data.title: Solutions
data.subtitle: Climate solutions

data.text: Pillar B recognizes companies whose products and services enable avoided emissions or generate negative emissions beyond their own footprint. It evaluates:

data.title: scale of impact through avoided/induced emissions ratio

data.text: This pillar integrates financial scale and actual climate mitigation impact.

data.title: Finance
data.subtitle: Climate finance

data.text:
Pillar C values voluntary financial contributions to climate mitigation — particularly those made outside the company's value chain.
It covers a broad spectrum of instruments, including:
Only voluntary contributions are included, and strict exclusions prevent double counting with operational decarbonization.

data.title: How scoring works
data.subtitle: From external frameworks to comparable scores.

data.title: Assessment
data.subtitle: Each sub-pillar is first assessed from recognized external framework results.

data.title: Translation
data.subtitle: These results — scores, ratings, labels, ratios, certifications — are converted to a normalized 0 to 100 scale via the CCF Translation Kit.

data.title: Adjustment
data.subtitle: They are then adjusted by two credibility factors: The assessment framework score and the assessment quality score.

data.text:
This mechanism ensures that companies are rewarded not only for their performance, but also for the rigor with which that performance is assessed.
If multiple frameworks apply to the same dimension, the highest score is retained — being assessed multiple times is therefore always beneficial.

data.title: Ensuring fairness across sectors.
data.subtitle: Different sectors play fundamentally distinct roles in the climate transition. The CCF derives specific sector coefficients for each pillar from activity classifications covering over 100 sub-sectors. This approach focuses assessment on the most material levers — and prevents any compensation between structurally different pillars.

data.title: Emissions intensity
data.subtitle: The weight of the Reduction pillar is proportional to the sector's carbon intensity across Scopes 1, 2, and 3. The more a sector emits, the more material the decarbonization of its operations.

data.title: Capacity to deploy solutions
data.subtitle: The weight of the Solutions pillar reflects the sector's technological potential to produce goods or services enabling avoided emissions by other actors. A battery manufacturer has a structurally higher β than a service provider.

data.title: Financial strength
data.subtitle: The weight of the Finance pillar integrates sector net margins and historical responsibility. It reflects the capacity — and obligation — to contribute financially to mitigation beyond one's own value chain.

data.text: This approach: focuses assessment on the most material levers for each sector, creates a level playing field across industries, and prevents compensation between structurally different pillars.

data.title: Making the framework operational // for all companies.
data.subtitle: The two tools ensuring broad adoption.

data.title: Translation Kit
data.subtitle: A conversion system that transforms heterogeneous results — numerical scores, levels, labels, ratios — into normalized results from 0 to 100. It makes assessments from very different framework natures directly comparable.

data.title: CCF Simplified Approach
data.subtitle: A structured maturity matrix assessment for companies not yet covered by external frameworks. It ensures universal applicability while applying lower credibility weights — and creates a documented progression pathway.

data.title: Three clear results: Potential, actual, and performance
data.subtitle: This structure answers both "What are you doing?" and "What could you do?", creating accountability while recognizing the diversity of pathways. The result: actionable insights — clear signals on areas where companies should invest and where capital can generate maximum impact.

data.title: Contribution Potential
data.subtitle: Maximum achievable contribution based on the sector's climate role

data.title: Actual Contribution
data.subtitle: Current performance across all three pillars

data.title: Contribution Performance
data.subtitle: Ratio between actual contribution and contribution potential

data.title: Actionable data // for markets and decision-makers.
data.subtitle: The CCF was designed to produce actionable results — not just scores. Each audience has a distinct use angle, adapted to their decision needs.

data.title: Companies
data.subtitle: To plan, report, and prioritize high-contribution impact actions. The CCF scorecard identifies the least exploited levers and directs efforts toward what matters most for the sector.

data.title: Investors
data.subtitle: To identify real climate leaders and allocate capital accordingly. The CCF's cross-sector comparability enables robust portfolio analysis, beyond footprint reduction trajectories alone.

data.title: Public decision-makers and NGOs
data.subtitle: To complement reporting obligations with an independent, robust, and comparable reference. The CCF provides an aggregated view of real corporate climate action, at the sector or economy level.

data.title: Want to understand // your role in the transition // to net zero?
data.subtitle: Start the 3-minute benchmark or download the White Paper to explore the methodology in detail.
